title: Woollen versus worsted draw
subtitle: The two fundamental drafting techniques and the yarn characters they produce
excerpt: The choice between woollen and worsted draw is the single biggest determinant of hand-spun yarn character. Woollen draw traps air in the yarn structure, producing a lofty, light, warm yarn; worsted draw compresses fibres parallel, producing a smooth, dense, hard-wearing yarn. Both methods work on a drop spindle or spinning wheel; the difference lies in how the fibre is prepared and how the drafting hand controls the fibre supply.
sourceNotes: The distinction between woollen and worsted draw is fundamental to hand-spinning instruction. Well documented in contemporary spinning reference texts and in historical weaving manuals. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A drafting technique in which the back hand holds the fibre supply and the front hand drafts forward, allowing air to be trapped between fibres as twist enters. Produces a lofty, airy yarn. Best with carded rolags.
An extended version of the woollen draw in which the back hand releases fibre over a long distance (30 to 60 cm) as the front hand controls twist entry. Fast and produces very lofty yarn.
The wraps-per-inch count that a spinner aims for to match a specific yarn weight. Setting a WPI target before spinning allows consistent production across multiple bobbins.

content.text: and the worsted draw are not just different techniques; they start from different fibre preparations. Carded rolags feed the woollen draw; combed top feeds the worsted draw. Applying a woollen draft to combed top produces a mediocre yarn of neither character. The right preparation for the intended yarn type is the first decision.

Use rolags or a lofty hand-carded preparation. The back hand holds the rolag. The front hand (closer to the wheel orifice) pinches the yarn between thumb and index finger to control where twist enters. The back hand releases fibre backward, away from the twist. Twist travels up to the front-hand pinch, then releases when the front hand opens. The
extends this principle: the back hand releases over 30 to 60 cm per stroke, producing yarn faster and with more air.

Use combed top. Both hands stay between the fibre supply and the wheel orifice. The front hand drafts by sliding forward along the fibres, drawing them out while the back hand provides a light counter-tension. Twist is kept behind the drafting zone by pinching with the front hand. The yarn is smooth and dense because no air is trapped.

Knitwear for warmth: woollen draw. Warp thread for weaving: worsted draw. A sock yarn that will take hard wear: worsted draw. A lace shawl that must be light: woollen draw. Outer garment in hard use (jacket, coat): worsted. Soft interior garment (jumper worn next to skin): depends on the fibre: fine merino woollen draw is soft enough; coarse border-Leicester worsted draw is still acceptable.

before beginning a batch. Spin 50 cm, wind it onto a ruler section, and count wraps. Adjust the drafting width and speed until the WPI reading is consistent across three test sections. Once set, maintain the drafting rhythm and do not significantly vary the fibre supply amount per inch.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems when switching between drafting methods.

items.symptom: Yarn is lumpy when using combed top with a woollen draw
items.cause: Woollen draw releases fibre in the wrong direction for parallel top fibres, creating clumps.
items.fix: Switch to worsted draw with combed top. The two are matched: woollen draw needs rolags; worsted draw needs top.

items.symptom: Worsted draw yarn is stiffer and harsher than expected
items.cause: The fibre was too coarse for wearing next to skin, or the yarn was over-twisted.
items.fix: Test fibre against the neck before spinning a large quantity. A high-twist worsted in 28-micron wool is still uncomfortable next to skin. Lower the twist ratio slightly and check the hand of the finished yarn.
